subject: Biology
text: All living cells have to be provided with nutrients, oxygen and other essential substances. Also, the waste or harmful substances produced, have to be removed continuously for healthy functioning of tissues. It is therefore, essential to have efficient mechanisms for the movement of these substances to the cells and from the cells. Blood is the most commonly used body fluid by most of the higher organisms, including humans, for this purpose. Another body fluid, lymph, also helps in the transport of certain substances. Blood is a special connective tissue consisting of a fluid matrix, plasma, and formed elements. Plasma is a straw coloured, viscous fluid constituting nearly 55 per cent of the blood. About 90-92 per cent of plasma is water and proteins contribute 6-8 per cent of it. Fibrinogen, globulins and albumins are the major proteins. Fibrinogens are needed for clotting or coagulation of blood. Globulins primarily are involved in defense mechanisms of the body and the albumins help in osmotic balance. Plasma also contains small amounts of minerals like sodium, calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate, and chloride ions. Glucose, amino acids, and lipids are also present in the plasma as they are always in transit in the body. Factors for coagulation of blood are also present in the plasma in an inactive form. Plasma without the clotting factors is called serum.

subject: Biology
text: Erythrocytes, leucocytes and platelets are collectively called formed elements and they constitute nearly 45 per cent of the blood. Erythrocytes or red blood cells (RBC) are the most abundant of all the cells in blood. A healthy adult man has, on an average, 5 millions to 5.5 millions of RBCs per cubic millimetre of blood. RBCs are formed in the red bone marrow in the adults. RBCs are devoid of nucleus in most of the mammals and are biconcave in shape. They have a red coloured, iron containing complex protein called haemoglobin, which gives the color and name to these cells. A healthy individual has 12-16 grams of haemoglobin in every 100 ml of blood. These molecules play a significant role in transport of respiratory gases. RBCs have an average life span of 120 days after which they are destroyed in the spleen, which is often referred to as the graveyard of RBCs. This constant production and destruction cycle ensures that the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood remains stable under normal physiological conditions.

subject: Biology
text: Leucocytes are also known as white blood cells (WBC) as they are colourless due to the lack of haemoglobin. They are nucleated and are relatively lesser in number, averaging 6000-8000 per cubic millimetre of blood. Leucocytes are generally short lived and are divided into two main categories: granulocytes and agranulocytes. Neutrophils, eosinophils and basophils are types of granulocytes, while lymphocytes and monocytes are agranulocytes. Neutrophils are the most abundant cells (60-65 per cent) and basophils are the least (0.5-1 per cent). Neutrophils and monocytes (6-8 per cent) are phagocytic cells which destroy foreign organisms entering the body. Basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, and heparin, and are involved in inflammatory reactions. Eosinophils (2-3 per cent) resist infections and are associated with allergic reactions. Lymphocytes (20-25 per cent) are of two major types—'B' and 'T' forms—both responsible for immune responses. Platelets, also called thrombocytes, are cell fragments produced from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. Blood normally contains 150,000 to 350,000 platelets per cubic millimetre. Platelets release substances involved in the coagulation or clotting of blood. A reduction in their number can lead to clotting disorders and excessive blood loss.

subject: Biology
text: The blood of human beings differs in certain aspects although it appears to be similar. Various types of grouping of blood have been established, with the ABO and Rh systems being the most widely used globally. ABO grouping is based on the presence or absence of two surface antigens on the RBCs, namely A and B. Similarly, the plasma of different individuals contains two natural antibodies, which are proteins produced in response to antigens. Based on these, blood is classified into four groups: A, B, AB and O. Group O blood lacks surface antigens and can be donated to persons with any other blood group, making those with O group 'universal donors'. Conversely, persons with group AB blood have both antigens but no antibodies in their plasma, allowing them to accept blood from any group; they are known as 'universal recipients'. During blood transfusion, it is critical that the blood of a donor is carefully matched with that of the recipient to avoid severe problems such as clumping or the destruction of RBCs, which can be life-threatening.

subject: Biology
text: Another important antigen is the Rh antigen, similar to one present in Rhesus monkeys, which is observed on the surface of RBCs in nearly 80 per cent of humans. Individuals possessing this antigen are Rh positive (Rh+ve), while those lacking it are Rh negative (Rh-ve). If an Rh-ve person is exposed to Rh+ve blood, they will develop specific antibodies against the Rh antigens. Therefore, Rh group matching is essential before blood transfusions. A special case of Rh incompatibility occurs between an Rh-ve pregnant mother and an Rh+ve foetus. While the two blood systems are usually separated by the placenta during the first pregnancy, exposure can occur during delivery. If the mother's blood is exposed to foetal Rh+ve blood, she begins preparing antibodies. In subsequent pregnancies, these Rh antibodies can leak into the foetus's blood and destroy its RBCs. This condition, known as erythroblastosis foetalis, can cause severe anaemia and jaundice in the baby. It can be prevented by administering anti-Rh antibodies to the mother immediately after the birth of the first child.

subject: Biology
text: Blood exhibits coagulation or clotting in response to an injury or trauma as a mechanism to prevent excessive blood loss. A dark reddish-brown scum, called a clot or coagulum, forms at the site of an injury over time. This clot consists mainly of a network of threads called fibrins, which trap dead and damaged formed elements of the blood. Fibrins are produced by the conversion of inactive fibrinogens in the plasma by the enzyme thrombin. Thrombins are themselves formed from another inactive substance in the plasma called prothrombin. This conversion requires an enzyme complex known as thrombokinase. The entire process involves a series of linked enzymic reactions, called a cascade process, which involves various factors present in the plasma in an inactive state. An injury or trauma stimulates platelets to release certain factors that activate this coagulation mechanism. Tissues at the site of injury can also release factors to initiate the process. Calcium ions play a vital and necessary role in the successful completion of the blood clotting process.

subject: Biology
text: As blood passes through tissue capillaries, water and small water-soluble substances move into the spaces between cells, leaving larger proteins and formed elements behind. This fluid is called interstitial fluid or tissue fluid and has a mineral distribution similar to plasma. It facilitates the exchange of nutrients and gases between the blood and the cells. The lymphatic system, an elaborate network of vessels, collects this fluid and drains it back into the major veins. The fluid within this system is called lymph. Lymph is a colourless fluid containing specialised lymphocytes responsible for the body's immune responses. It also serves as a carrier for nutrients and hormones. Fats are specifically absorbed through lymph in specialized structures called lacteals found in the intestinal villi. Broadly, circulatory patterns in animals are either open or closed. In an open system, found in arthropods and molluscs, blood is pumped by the heart into body cavities called sinuses. In a closed system, seen in annelids and chordates, blood is always circulated through a network of vessels, allowing for more precise regulation of fluid flow.

subject: Biology
text: All vertebrates possess a muscular chambered heart, but the number of chambers varies by class. Fishes have a two-chambered heart with one atrium and one ventricle, where the heart pumps deoxygenated blood that is oxygenated by the gills (single circulation). Amphibians and most reptiles possess a three-chambered heart with two atria and a single ventricle. In this setup, the left atrium receives oxygenated blood while the right atrium receives deoxygenated blood, but they mix in the single ventricle (incomplete double circulation). Crocodiles, birds, and mammals have a four-chambered heart with two atria and two ventricles. In these organisms, oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are received by the left and right atria respectively and passed to the corresponding ventricles. The ventricles then pump the blood out without any mixing, creating two separate circulatory pathways known as double circulation. This evolution toward separated chambers allows for a more efficient supply of oxygen to the body tissues, which is particularly important for homeothermic animals that have high metabolic demands.

subject: Biology
text: The human circulatory system consists of a muscular chambered heart, a network of closed branching blood vessels, and blood. The heart is a mesodermally derived organ situated in the thoracic cavity between the lungs, slightly tilted to the left. It is roughly the size of a clenched fist and is protected by a double-walled membranous bag called the pericardium, which encloses pericardial fluid. The heart has four chambers: two small upper chambers called atria and two larger lower chambers called ventricles. A thin muscular wall, the inter-atrial septum, separates the atria, while a thick-walled inter-ventricular septum separates the ventricles. Fibrous tissue called the atrio-ventricular septum separates the atrium and ventricle on the same side, but contains openings for blood flow. The opening between the right atrium and ventricle is guarded by the tricuspid valve (three flaps), while the bicuspid or mitral valve guards the opening on the left side. Semilunar valves are located at the openings of the ventricles into the pulmonary artery and aorta, ensuring blood flows only in one direction and preventing backward flow.

subject: Biology
text: The entire heart is composed of cardiac muscles, with the walls of the ventricles being much thicker than those of the atria. A specialized cardiac musculature called nodal tissue is distributed throughout the heart. One patch, the sino-atrial node (SAN), is located in the right upper corner of the right atrium. Another mass, the atrio-ventricular node (AVN), is found in the lower left corner of the right atrium near the septum. A bundle of nodal fibres, the atrio-ventricular bundle (AV bundle), continues from the AVN, passes through the septa, and divides into right and left bundles. these give rise to minute fibres called Purkinje fibres throughout the ventricular walls. The nodal musculature is autoexcitable, meaning it can generate action potentials without external stimuli. The SAN generates the highest number of action potentials (70-75 per minute) and is responsible for initiating and maintaining the heart's rhythmic contractile activity. For this reason, the SAN is called the pacemaker. Under normal conditions, the heart beats 70-75 times per minute, with an average rate of 72 beats per minute.

subject: Biology
text: To understand heart function, we look at the cardiac cycle. Initially, all four chambers are in a relaxed state known as joint diastole. Blood flows from the vena cava and pulmonary veins into the right and left ventricles through the open tricuspid and bicuspid valves, while the semilunar valves are closed. The SAN then generates an action potential, causing the atria to contract (atrial systole), which increases ventricular blood flow by about 30 per cent. This action potential is then conducted to the ventricles via the AVN and AV bundle (Bundle of His), causing the ventricular muscles to contract (ventricular systole) while the atria relax (atrial diastole). The rising ventricular pressure closes the tricuspid and bicuspid valves, preventing backflow. As pressure increases further, the semilunar valves are forced open, allowing blood to flow into the pulmonary artery and aorta. As the ventricles relax (ventricular diastole), pressure falls, causing the semilunar valves to close to prevent backflow from the arteries. Finally, the tricuspid and bicuspid valves open again as atrial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure, and the cycle repeats.

subject: Biology
text: The cardiac cycle is a sequential event consisting of systole and diastole of both atria and ventricles. Given a heart rate of 72 beats per minute, the duration of a single cardiac cycle is approximately 0.8 seconds. During each cycle, each ventricle pumps out about 70 mL of blood, which is referred to as the stroke volume. The cardiac output is determined by multiplying the stroke volume by the heart rate (number of beats per minute). For a healthy individual, the cardiac output averages 5000 mL or 5 litres per minute. The body has the capacity to alter both the stroke volume and the heart rate to change the cardiac output according to demand; for example, the cardiac output of an athlete is significantly higher than that of an ordinary person. During each cardiac cycle, two distinct heart sounds are produced that can be heard via a stethoscope. The first sound, 'lub', is associated with the closure of the tricuspid and bicuspid valves at the start of ventricular systole. The second sound, 'dub', is associated with the closure of the semilunar valves at the start of ventricular diastole.

subject: Biology
text: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is a graphical representation of the electrical activity of the heart during a cardiac cycle. In a standard setup, a patient is connected to the machine with three electrical leads—one to each wrist and one to the left ankle—to monitor heart activity. Each peak in the ECG is labeled from P to T. The P-wave represents the electrical excitation or depolarisation of the atria, which leads to atrial contraction. The QRS complex represents the depolarisation of the ventricles, which initiates ventricular contraction. This contraction begins shortly after the Q wave and marks the beginning of systole. The T-wave represents the repolarisation of the ventricles as they return from an excited to a normal state. The end of the T-wave signifies the end of systole. By counting the number of QRS complexes in a given time period, one can determine the heart rate. Because standard ECGs have a consistent shape among individuals, any deviation from this pattern can indicate potential heart abnormalities or diseases, making it a vital clinical diagnostic tool.

subject: Biology
text: Blood flows through the body via a fixed route of arteries and veins. These vessels consist of three layers: an inner squamous endothelium (tunica intima), a middle layer of smooth muscle and elastic fibres (tunica media), and an outer layer of fibrous connective tissue (tunica externa). The tunica media is notably thinner in veins. Double circulation involves two distinct pathways. The pulmonary circulation begins when deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle is pumped into the pulmonary artery, travels to the lungs for oxygenation, and returns to the left atrium via the pulmonary veins. The systemic circulation starts with oxygenated blood from the left ventricle being pumped into the aorta, which distributes it through a network of arteries and capillaries to the tissues. Deoxygenated blood is then collected by venules and veins, eventually reaching the right atrium through the vena cava. Systemic circulation delivers nutrients and oxygen while removing carbon dioxide and waste. Specialized connections also exist, such as the hepatic portal system, which carries blood from the intestine to the liver, and the coronary system, which supplies the heart muscle itself.

subject: Biology
text: The heart's normal activities are regulated intrinsically by specialized muscles known as nodal tissue, which is why the heart is described as myogenic. However, the cardiac function can be moderated by the neural system. A specialized center in the medulla oblongata regulates heart activity through the autonomic nervous system (ANS). The sympathetic nerves, a part of the ANS, can send signals to increase the heart rate, strengthen ventricular contraction, and thereby increase cardiac output. Conversely, parasympathetic neural signals decrease the heart rate and the speed of action potential conduction, which reduces the cardiac output. Hormonal control also plays a role; for instance, adrenal medullary hormones can be released to increase cardiac output during stress or physical activity. This dual regulation ensures that the heart can rapidly adjust its output to meet the changing metabolic needs of the body, whether at rest or during intense physical exertion, maintaining homeostasis throughout the organism.

subject: Biology
text: Several disorders can affect the human circulatory system. Hypertension, or high blood pressure, occurs when blood pressure is consistently higher than the normal 120/80 mm Hg. The first number (120) represents systolic or pumping pressure, while the second (80) represents diastolic or resting pressure. Blood pressure readings of 140/90 or higher indicate hypertension, which can lead to heart disease and damage vital organs like the brain and kidneys. Coronary Artery Disease (CAD), also known as atherosclerosis, involves the narrowing of the arteries supplying the heart muscle due to deposits of calcium, fat, and cholesterol. Angina (angina pectoris) is characterized by acute chest pain resulting from an insufficient supply of oxygen to the heart muscle. Heart failure occurs when the heart cannot pump blood effectively enough to meet the body's needs. It is often called congestive heart failure due to associated lung congestion. It is distinct from cardiac arrest, where the heart stops beating, and a heart attack, where the heart muscle is suddenly damaged by inadequate blood supply.
